---
title: "Applied public-health GIS — health department practicum"
subtitle: "Spatial analysis and surveillance analytics for a local health department"
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Applied epidemiology]{.tag} [ArcGIS]{.tag} [Space-time analysis]{.tag} [Surveillance data]{.tag} [Public health]{.tag}
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Data-sensitive details are omitted. This describes methods and deliverables at a
level that discloses nothing about individuals or protected data.
:::

## Problem

Small health departments generate a lot of data — case reports, immunization
records, addresses — but rarely have the capacity to turn it into spatial
analysis that can guide where to put limited resources. Two concrete needs came
up during my MPH practicum: leadership wanted to know *where* respiratory
illness was clustering across the jurisdiction, and the immunization program
needed to know which patients were overdue for required doses so nurses could
follow up.

## Approach

Take existing operational data, clean and structure it into something
analyzable, run appropriate spatial or record-level analysis, and hand back a
deliverable the staff can re-run and act on — a notebook, a map, or a report —
along with a plain-language note on what it does and doesn't show.

## Methods and tools

**Respiratory illness spatial analysis**

- QA and structuring of a 3,454-record case export (2015–2026) into an
  analyzable dataset.
- A reusable, time-enabled hex-binned count workflow so the analysis can be
  re-run by changing a single input.
- Space-time cube construction and **emerging hot-spot analysis** to distinguish
  persistent or intensifying trends from one-off spikes.
- Getis-Ord Gi\* hot- and cold-spot analysis by time period.
- A standalone HTML web map and a plain-language method note for non-technical
  staff.
- Built with ArcGIS Pro and `arcpy`.

**Immunization-compliance reporting**

- A specification mapping required vaccines to dose groups and age-tiered
  requirements, including the exception rules.
- Calculated fields for per-group dose counts, missing doses, and school-entry
  compliance status, built on EHR-exported data.
- A missed-dose patient list for outreach, and groundwork for mapping
  vaccine-uptake patterns from geocoded addresses.
- QA and logic-verification steps documented alongside the build — including
  catching a report-duplication issue in the EHR export that was inflating dose
  counts, and documenting it so the numbers weren't used for outreach before the
  fix.

## Status

The respiratory-illness analysis was completed and handed off, and was used
operationally to help plan outreach. The immunization-compliance work was in
progress during the practicum period.

## What this demonstrates

- Turning messy operational public-health data into reproducible analysis.
- Correctly applying space-time and local-cluster methods — and being careful
  about the difference between a raw count spike and a statistical trend.
- Building deliverables that non-technical staff can run and use, with honest
  method notes.
- Handling sensitive data responsibly.
